raw_text: Scope of Academic Freedom Academic staff and students have the right to question and test received wisdom, put forward new ideas, and express controversial or unpopular opinions within the law. This right is protected provided it does not constitute harassment, incitement, or unlawful discrimination

External Speakers Invitations to external speakers must be submitted to the university for approval at least 14 days before the event. Events featuring speakers who may express views that risk public order or constitute hate speech require enhanced risk assessment. Unauthorized events may be cancelled without notice

Protest and Demonstration Peaceful protest on university premises is permitted within designated areas and requires advance notification to security. Protests that disrupt teaching, research, or administrative activities will be dispersed and participants may face disciplinary action

Social Media and Public Comment Staff and students may comment publicly on matters of academic and public interest. However, public statements that misrepresent the university's position, disclose confidential information, or constitute harassment are not protected by academic freedom principles
Retaliation Retaliation against any member of the university community for exercising legitimate academic freedom or freedom of expression is prohibited and constitutes misconduct

Initiation of Investigation An academic misconduct investigation is initiated upon receipt of a formal report from a faculty member, invigilator, or another student. All reports must be submitted in writing within 10 working days of the alleged incident. Late reports require justification and may not be accepted

Notification The accused student will be notified in writing within 5 working days of the investigation being opened. Failure to respond to notification within 10 working days will result in the investigation proceeding in the student's absence

Evidence Gathering The investigating officer will gather all relevant evidence including submitted work, examination scripts, digital logs, and witness statements. Destruction or tampering with evidence constitutes a further act of misconduct and may result in enhanced penalties

Hearing A formal hearing will be convened within 20 working days. The student has the right to attend, present their case, and be accompanied by a student union representative. Refusal to attend without valid reason is treated as an admission

Respectful Conduct Students must treat all members of the learning community with respect. Disruptive, abusive, or threatening behaviour in class will result in removal from the session and formal disciplinary action

Recording Policy Recording of lectures or seminars without explicit instructor permission is prohibited. Unauthorised recordings that are shared publicly constitute a serious breach of privacy and intellectual property rights

Technology Use Mobile phones must be silenced during lectures. Use of laptops or tablets is permitted for academic purposes only. Repeated misuse of technology after warning may result in prohibition from device use during class
Attendance Attendance at all scheduled teaching sessions is expected. Students with attendance below the required threshold may be barred from assessments or referred to the academic support team

Academic Accommodations Students requiring reasonable adjustments must register with the Disability Services Office. Adjustments cannot be applied retroactively to completed assessments under any circumstances
All scholarship teaching, research, and student learning is the product of intellectual exchange

Whether this exchange takes place in books and journal articles, in laboratories, in the design of experiments and the analysis of data, in the classroom, or in students written work, it is these joint undertakings that create Columbia s intellectual community at Columbia College
The value of our collective inquiry relies upon trust and honesty for our individual discoveries are dependent upon the discoveries of our peers and predecessors, here at Columbia and elsewhere
And all intellectual work must be evaluated the work of students is evaluated by faculty; the work of faculty is evaluated through peer-review
We must, therefore, be able to trust that others are honest in their work and others must be able to trust that we are honest in ours

The Columbia College Student Council, on behalf of the whole student body, has resolved that maintaining academic integrity is the preserve of all members of our intellectual community including and especially students
As a consequence, all Columbia College students make the following pledge
In addition, all Columbia College students are committed to the following honor code
I affirm that I will not plagiarize, use unauthorized materials, or give or receive illegitimate help on assignments, papers, or examinations
I will also uphold equity and honesty in the evaluation of my work and the work of others
I do so to sustain a community built around this Code of Honor
For more information on the ways that College and SEAS students are introduced to, and trained on, matters of academic integrity, visit the Academic Integrity website for undergraduates
The intellectual venture in which we are all engaged requires of faculty and students alike the highest level of personal and academic integrity

As members of an academic community, each one of us bears the responsibility to participate in scholarly discourse and research in a manner characterized by intellectual honesty and scholarly integrity
Scholarship, by its very nature, is an iterative process, with ideas and insights building one upon the other
Collaborative scholarship requires the study of other scholars' work, the free discussion of such work, and the explicit acknowledgement of those ideas in any work that inform our own
This exchange of ideas relies upon a mutual trust that sources, opinions, facts, and insights will be properly noted and carefully credited

In practical terms, this means that, as students, you must be responsible for the full citations of others' ideas in all of your research papers and projects; you must be scrupulously honest when taking your examinations; you must always submit your own work and not that of another student, scholar, or internet agent
Any breach of this intellectual responsibility is a breach of faith with the rest of our academic community
It undermines our shared intellectual culture, and it cannot be tolerated
Students failing to meet these responsibilities should anticipate being asked to leave Columbia

For more information on academic integrity at Columbia, students may refer to the Columbia University Undergraduate Guide to Academic Integrity : http://www.college.columbia.edu/academics/academicintegrity

Minimum Passing Grade: 50%. Students failing to achieve 50% must retake the course and may not proceed to dependent courses until the requirement is met
Academic Honesty All submitted work must represent the student's own original effort
Unauthorized use of AI tools, copying from other students, or submitting previously graded work is a violation of the Academic Integrity Policy
Submission Policy All assignments must be submitted through the official course portal before 11:59 PM on the due date
Emailed submissions are not accepted unless the portal is unavailable, in which case email submission must be accompanied by a support ticket reference number

Communication Students must check the course email account within 48 hours. Failure to respond to official communications regarding academic standing may result in administrative holds on enrollment and registration

Withdrawal Students wishing to withdraw must do so before the published withdrawal deadline. Unofficial withdrawal after the deadline results in an automatic F grade recorded on the permanent academic transcript

Submission Requirements Students must submit their dissertation by the published deadline. Late submission without an approved extension results in a grade penalty of one grade boundary per week of delay. Submission more than four weeks late without approval results in automatic failure

Originality Requirement All dissertations must be the student's own original work. Plagiarism, contract cheating, or submission of AI-generated content as original work will result in automatic failure of the dissertation and referral to the Academic Misconduct Committee

Word Count Students must adhere to the prescribed word count. Submissions exceeding the limit by more than 10% may be penalised. Submissions below 80% of the minimum word count will not be accepted for assessment

Ethical Compliance Dissertations involving human participants, sensitive data, or secondary data must include evidence of ethics approval. Submission without required ethical clearance will not be processed
Viva Voce Students may be required to defend their dissertation in an oral examination. Failure to attend a scheduled viva without prior notice constitutes a fail for the dissertation module

Academic Integrity Students must not engage in any form of academic dishonesty including plagiarism, fabrication, cheating, contract cheating, or unauthorized collaboration. Violations are treated as serious misconduct and may result in course failure, suspension, or permanent expulsion from the university. All violations are recorded on the student's academic file

Examination Conduct Mobile phones and unauthorized electronic devices are strictly prohibited during examinations. Students found in possession of unauthorized materials shall receive a grade of zero for the entire examination and be referred to the Academic Integrity Committee for formal investigation

Plagiarism Presenting another person's work as one's own, whether intentional or unintentional, constitutes plagiarism. Confirmed plagiarism results in a failing grade for the assessment. Repeat offences result in academic suspension for a minimum of one semester

Use of AI Tools Submission of AI-generated text as original work without disclosure is considered academic dishonesty. First offences result in assignment failure. Repeat offences result in course failure and referral to the Disciplinary Committee

Late Submission Assignments submitted after the deadline without approved extension receive a deduction of 10% per calendar day. No submission accepted after 7 days without documented medical or compassionate grounds
Attendance Students must attend at least 75% of scheduled classes. Students below minimum attendance are barred from final examinations without appeal
Board Authority The Examination Board is the sole body authorised to confirm student results, awards, and academic progression decisions. No individual member of staff may override a Board decision

Extenuating Circumstances Claims for extenuating circumstances must be submitted before the assessment or within 5 working days after. Late claims without documented justification will not be considered by the Board

Repeat Assessments Students who fail a module are entitled to one further attempt at the failed component. A second failure results in module failure with no further attempts permitted. Students failing core modules may not progress to the next level

Award Classification Attempts to influence award classification through improper means including correspondence with Board members constitute academic misconduct subject to disciplinary action and potential withdrawal of the award

Transcript Accuracy Students must report any errors in their academic transcript within 30 days of issue. Alterations to official transcripts by any party other than the Registry constitute fraud and are subject to legal action

Risk Assessment All off-campus activities must have a completed and approved risk assessment before departure. Conducting a field trip without an approved risk assessment is prohibited and constitutes a serious health and safety violation

Student Consent Participation in field trips requires signed informed consent. Students who participate without submitting a valid consent form may be required to leave the activity at their own expense

Code of Conduct Students on field trips represent the university and must comply with the student code of conduct at all times. Misconduct on field trips is subject to the same disciplinary procedures as on-campus misconduct

Alcohol and Substances Consumption of alcohol by students under the legal drinking age during field trips is strictly prohibited. Possession or use of illegal substances during any university activity results in immediate removal and formal disciplinary proceedings

Accidents and Incidents All accidents, near-misses, and incidents during field trips must be reported to the trip leader immediately and documented. Failure to report incidents may affect insurance coverage and constitutes a breach of university health and safety policy

Entry Requirements Applicants who do not meet published entry requirements will not be admitted. Fraudulent or misrepresented qualifications will result in immediate withdrawal of any offer and termination of enrolment if discovered post-admission
Conditional Offers Conditional offers lapse if conditions are not met by the published deadline. Requests for extensions are considered only in exceptional documented circumstances
Deferral Policy Deferral of entry is permitted for a maximum of one academic year. A second deferral request will not be granted and the applicant must reapply
Enrolment Students must complete enrolment by the published deadline. Failure to enrol without notification results in automatic withdrawal of the place
False Declaration Providing false information on any application or enrolment document is grounds for immediate withdrawal of admission and may result in legal action

Visa Compliance International students must maintain valid immigration status at all times. Failure to comply with visa conditions may result in reporting to immigration authorities and termination of enrollment

Attendance Monitoring International students are subject to mandatory attendance monitoring. Absence below 70% of required contact hours triggers a mandatory welfare review and may result in reporting to immigration authorities

English Language Requirements Students who do not meet minimum English proficiency standards after enrollment may be required to undertake additional language support. Failure to meet minimum standards after support may result in suspension from assessments

Fee Payment International tuition fees must be paid by the published deadlines. Non-payment results in removal from course registration and may affect visa status. Outstanding fees must be settled before any academic transcript is released

IT Systems Access University IT systems are provided for academic and administrative use only. Unauthorized use, access to restricted areas, or attempts to compromise system security are serious violations subject to disciplinary action and may result in criminal prosecution under applicable computer misuse laws

Software and Copyright Installation of unlicensed software on university equipment is prohibited. Downloading or distributing copyrighted materials without authorization constitutes copyright infringement and subjects the student to legal liability

Library Resources Library materials must be returned by the due date. Overdue materials attract fines. Theft or deliberate damage to library resources is a disciplinary offence that may result in suspension of library privileges and financial restitution

Data and Privacy Students must not access, share, or modify other users' data without authorization. Unauthorized access to another person's files or accounts constitutes a serious breach of privacy and university policy, subject to immediate disciplinary action

Loan Periods Standard loan items may be borrowed for 3 weeks and renewed twice online. Items recalled by another user must be returned within 5 working days of notification. Failure to return recalled items results in suspension of borrowing privileges

Overdue Fines Overdue fines accrue daily. Outstanding fines above a threshold result in suspension of library and IT services. Unpaid fines may be referred to the finance department and added to student accounts

Damage and Loss Students are responsible for all items borrowed. Damaged or lost items must be reported immediately. Students will be charged the full replacement cost plus a processing fee. Deliberate damage is a disciplinary offence

Special Collections Special collections and archive materials may only be used in designated areas under supervision. Removal of special collection items from the designated area is prohibited and constitutes theft subject to disciplinary and legal action
Academic Integrity Photocopying beyond the permitted fair use limits and sharing library access credentials are prohibited and may constitute copyright infringement

Identity Verification Students participating in online assessments must verify their identity as required. Allowing another person to complete an online assessment on one's behalf constitutes contract cheating and will result in expulsion

Assessment Integrity Online assessments must be completed independently unless group work is explicitly permitted. Use of unauthorized resources, websites, or AI tools during timed online assessments is treated as examination misconduct

Technical Issues Students experiencing technical issues during timed assessments must notify the module leader within 30 minutes of the issue occurring. Technical issues reported after assessment submission will not be considered as grounds for appeal

Recording of Sessions Online teaching sessions may be recorded for students who are unable to attend. Students must not share recordings outside the enrolled cohort. Redistribution of teaching recordings constitutes a breach of intellectual property rights

Participation Requirements Students must participate actively in online discussions and group activities as required by their programme. Consistent failure to participate may be treated as non-engagement and reported accordingly
Information about what plagiarism is, and how you can avoid it

The University defines plagiarism as follows: Presenting work or ideas from another source as your own, with or without consent of the original author, by incorporating it into your work without full acknowledgement

All published and unpublished material, whether in manuscript, printed or electronic form, is covered under this definition, as is the use of material generated wholly or in part through use of artificial intelligence (save when use of AI for assessment has received prior authorisation e.g. as a reasonable adjustment for a student s disability)
Plagiarism can also include re-using your own work without citation
Under the regulations for examinations, intentional or reckless plagiarism is a disciplinary offence
You must also attribute text, data, or other resources downloaded from websites

Please note that artificial intelligence (AI) can only be used within assessments where specific prior authorisation has been given, or when technology that uses AI has been agreed as reasonable adjustment for a student s disability (such as voice recognition software for transcriptions, or spelling and grammar checkers)
You should acknowledge your use of AI as part of the assessment submission and use a formal declaration in the format prescribed by the assessment setter
The best way of avoiding plagiarism is to learn and employ the principles of good academic practice from the beginning of your university career

Avoiding plagiarism is not simply a matter of making sure your references are all correct, or changing enough words so the examiner will not notice your paraphrase; it is about deploying your academic skills to make your work as good as it can be
Students will benefit from taking an online course which has been developed to provide a useful overview of the issues surrounding plagiarism and practical ways to avoid it

Verbatim (word for word) quotation without clear acknowledgement Quotations must always be identified as such by the use of either quotation marks or indentation, and with full referencing of the sources cited
It must always be apparent to the reader which parts are your own independent work and where you have drawn on ideas and language from another source
Cutting and pasting from the Internet without clear acknowledgement Information derived from the Internet must be adequately referenced and included in the bibliography
It is important to evaluate carefully all material found on the Internet, as it is less likely to have been through the same process of scholarly peer review as published sources

Paraphrasing Paraphrasing the work of others by altering a few words and changing their order, or by closely following the structure of their argument, is plagiarism if you do not give due acknowledgement to the author whose work you are using

A passing reference to the original author in your own text may not be enough; you must ensure that you do not create the misleading impression that the paraphrased wording or the sequence of ideas are entirely your own
It is better to write a brief summary of the author s overall argument in your own words, indicating that you are doing so, than to paraphrase particular sections of his or her writing
This will ensure you have a genuine grasp of the argument and will avoid the difficulty of paraphrasing without plagiarising
You must also properly attribute all material you derive from lectures
Collusion This can involve unauthorised collaboration between students, failure to attribute assistance received, or failure to follow precisely regulations on group work projects
It is your responsibility to ensure that you are entirely clear about the extent of collaboration permitted, and which parts of the work must be your own
Inaccurate citation It is important to cite correctly, according to the conventions of your discipline
As well as listing your sources (i.e. in a bibliography), you must indicate, using a footnote or an in-text reference, where a quoted passage comes from
Additionally, you should not include anything in your references or bibliography that you have not actually consulted

If you cannot gain access to a primary source you must make it clear in your citation that your knowledge of the work has been derived from a secondary text (for example, Bradshaw, D. Title of Book, discussed in Wilson, E., Title of Book (London, 2004), p. 189)

Failure to acknowledge assistance You must clearly acknowledge all assistance which has contributed to the production of your work, such as advice from fellow students, laboratory technicians, and other external sources

This need not apply to the assistance provided by your tutor or supervisor, or to ordinary proofreading, but it is necessary to acknowledge other guidance which leads to substantive changes of content or approach

Use of material written by professional agencies or other persons You should neither make use of professional agencies in the production of your work nor submit material which has been written for you even with the consent of the person who has written it
It is vital to your intellectual training and development that you should undertake the research process unaided
Under Statute XI on University Discipline, all members of the University are prohibited from providing material that could be submitted in an examination by students at this University or elsewhere

Auto-plagiarism You must not submit work for assessment that you have already submitted (partially or in full), either for your current course or for another qualification of this, or any other, university, unless this is specifically provided for in the special regulations for your course
Where earlier work by you is citable, i.e. it has already been published, you must reference it clearly
Identical pieces of work submitted concurrently will also be considered to be auto-plagiarism

It is a principle of intellectual honesty that all members of the academic community should acknowledge their debt to the originators of the ideas, words, and data which form the basis for their own work
Passing off another s work as your own is not only poor scholarship, but also means that you have failed to complete the learning process
Plagiarism is unethical and can have serious consequences for your future career; it also undermines the standards of your institution and of the degrees it issues
You have come to university to learn to know and speak your own mind, not merely to reproduce the opinions of others - at least not without attribution
At first it may seem very difficult to develop your own views, and you will probably find yourself paraphrasing the writings of others as you attempt to understand and assimilate their arguments
However it is important that you learn to develop your own voice

You are not necessarily expected to become an original thinker, but you are expected to be an independent one - by learning to assess critically the work of others, weigh up differing arguments and draw your own conclusions
You should avoid plagiarism because you aspire to produce work of the highest quality
Once you have grasped the principles of source use and citation, you should find it relatively straightforward to steer clear of plagiarism
Moreover, you will reap the additional benefits of improvements to both the lucidity and quality of your writing

It is important to appreciate that mastery of the techniques of academic writing is not merely a practical skill, but one that lends both credibility and authority to your work, and demonstrates your commitment to the principle of intellectual honesty in scholarship
Cases will be investigated and penalties may range from deduction of marks to expulsion from the University, depending on the seriousness of the occurrence
Even if plagiarism is inadvertent, it can result in a penalty
The forms of plagiarism listed above are all potentially disciplinary offences in the context of formal assessment requirements

The regulations regarding conduct in examinations apply equally to the submission and assessment of a thesis, dissertation, essay, or other coursework not undertaken in formal examination conditions but which counts towards or constitutes the work for a degree or other academic award
Additionally, this includes the transfer and confirmation of status exercises undertaken by graduate students
Cases of suspected plagiarism in assessed work are investigated under the disciplinary regulations concerning conduct in examinations
Intentional plagiarism in this context means that you understood that you were breaching the regulations and did so intending to gain advantage in the examination

Reckless, in this context, means that you understood or could be expected to have understood (even if you did not specifically consider it) that your work might breach the regulations, but you took no action to avoid doing so
Intentional or reckless plagiarism may incur severe penalties, including failure of your degree or expulsion from the university
If plagiarism is suspected in a piece of work submitted for assessment in an examination, the matter will be referred to the Proctors
They will thoroughly investigate the claim and call the student concerned for interview
If at this point there is no evidence of a breach of the regulations, no further disciplinary action will be taken although there may still be an academic penalty
However, if it is concluded that a breach of the regulations may have occurred, the Proctors will refer the case to the Student Disciplinary Panel

If you are suspected of plagiarism your College Secretary/Academic Administrator and subject tutor will support you through the process and arrange for a member of Congregation to accompany you to all hearings
They will be able to advise you what to expect during the investigation and how best to make your case
The Oxford SU Student Advice Service can also provide useful information and support
Does this mean that I shouldn t use the work of other authors?
On the contrary, it is vital that you situate your writing within the intellectual debates of your discipline
Academic essays almost always involve the use and discussion of material written by others, and, with due acknowledgement and proper referencing, this is clearly distinguishable from plagiarism
The knowledge in your discipline has developed cumulatively as a result of years of research, innovation and debate
You need to give credit to the authors of the ideas and observations you cite
Not only does this accord recognition to their work, it also helps you to strengthen your argument by making clear the basis on which you make it
Moreover, good citation practice gives your reader the opportunity to follow up your references, or check the validity of your interpretation
Does every statement in my essay have to be backed up with references?
You may feel that including the citation for every point you make will interrupt the flow of your essay and make it look very unoriginal
However, by employing good citation practice from the start, you will learn to avoid errors such as close paraphrasing or inadequately referenced quotation
It is important to understand the reasons behind the need for transparency of source use
All academic texts, even student essays, are multi-voiced, which means they are filled with references to other texts
Rather than attempting to synthesise these voices into one narrative account, you should make it clear whose interpretation or argument you are employing at any one time - whose voice is speaking

If you are substantially indebted to a particular argument in the formulation of your own, you should make this clear both in footnotes and in the body of your text according to the agreed conventions of the discipline, before going on to describe how your own views develop or diverge from this influence
On the other hand, it is not necessary to give references for facts that are common knowledge in your discipline
If you are unsure as to whether something is considered to be common knowledge or not, it is safer to cite it anyway and seek clarification
You do need to document facts that are not generally known and ideas that are interpretations of facts
Although plagiarism in weekly essays does not constitute a University disciplinary offence, it may well lead to College disciplinary measures
Persistent academic under-performance can even result in your being sent down from the University

Although tutorial essays traditionally do not require the full scholarly apparatus of footnotes and referencing, it is still necessary to acknowledge your sources and demonstrate the development of your argument, usually by an in-text reference
Many tutors will ask that you do employ a formal citation style early on, and you will find that this is good preparation for later project and dissertation work
In any case, your work will benefit considerably if you adopt good scholarly habits from the start, together with the techniques of critical thinking and writing described above
This does not mean that you must become masters of jargon and obfuscation; however the process is akin to learning a new language
It is necessary not only to learn new terminology, but the practical study skills and other techniques which will help you to learn effectively

Developing these skills throughout your time at university will not only help you to produce better coursework, dissertations, projects and exam papers, but will lay the intellectual foundations for your future career

Even if you have no intention of becoming an academic, being able to analyse evidence, exercise critical judgement, and write clearly and persuasively are skills that will serve you for life, and which any employer will value
Borrowing essays from other students to adapt and submit as your own is plagiarism, and will develop none of these necessary skills, holding back your academic development
Students who lend essays for this purpose are doing their peers no favours
Sometimes students may omit to take down citation details when taking notes, or they may be genuinely ignorant of referencing conventions
Even in cases where the plagiarism is found to have been neither intentional nor reckless, there may still be an academic penalty for poor practice
It is your responsibility to find out the prevailing referencing conventions in your discipline, to take adequate notes, and to avoid close paraphrasing
If you are offered induction sessions on plagiarism and study skills, you should attend
Together with the advice contained in your subject handbook, these will help you learn how to avoid common errors
If you are undertaking a project or dissertation you should ensure that you have information on plagiarism and collusion
If ever in doubt about referencing, paraphrasing or plagiarism, you have only to ask your tutor
There are some helpful examples of plagiarism-by-paraphrase and you will also find extensive advice on the referencing and library skills pages
These examples use the referencing system prescribed by the History Faculty but should be of use to students of all disciplines
In aspiring to that proud, if temporary, status of Gentleman of the Road , they did not question the inegalitarian hierarchy of their society

Yet their boldness of act and deed, in putting them outside the law as rebellious fugitives, revivified the animal spirits of capitalism and became an essential part of the oppositional culture of working-class London, a serious obstacle to the formation of a tractable, obedient labour force
Therefore, it was not enough to hang them the values they espoused or represented had to be challenged
[You should give the reference in full the first time you use it in a footnote; thereafter it is acceptable to use an abbreviated version, e.g. Linebaugh, The London Hanged, p. 213.]

Although they did not question the inegalitarian hierarchy of their society, highwaymen became an essential part of the oppositional culture of working-class London, posing a serious threat to the formation of a biddable labour force
(This is a patchwork of phrases copied verbatim from the source, with just a few words changed here and there
There is no reference to the original author and no indication that these words are not the writer s own.)
Although they did not question the inegalitarian hierarchy of their society, highwaymen exercised a powerful attraction for the working classes
Some historians believe that this hindered the development of a submissive workforce

As academic dishonesty rises at other universities, the Honor Pledge is a commitment to maintain a higher standard at Purdue

We are passionate about Purdue and believe in the students and faculty that make this university world renowned
Together, we can ensure Purdue’s reputation of excellence, both in and out of the classroom, is continued

Research Misconduct Research misconduct includes fabrication, falsification, and plagiarism (FFP). Any researcher found guilty of research misconduct shall face immediate suspension from research activities, retraction of published work, and may be subject to termination of employment or expulsion. Cases of serious misconduct are referred to relevant professional bodies and funding agencies

Data Management All research data must be stored securely and retained for a minimum of 10 years after publication. Destruction of research data to avoid scrutiny constitutes research misconduct and is subject to disciplinary action
Authorship Authorship must reflect genuine intellectual contribution. Gift authorship and ghost authorship are prohibited and constitute a form of academic misconduct subject to formal investigation

Conflict of Interest Researchers must disclose all financial and personal conflicts of interest before commencing research. Failure to disclose conflicts may result in retraction of findings and disciplinary action

Ethical Approval All research involving human subjects must receive ethics committee approval before commencement. Conducting research without approval is a serious violation subject to immediate suspension and potential legal action

Eligibility Maintenance Scholarships are awarded subject to continued satisfactory academic performance. Failure to maintain the required GPA or credit load will result in suspension or withdrawal of the scholarship without prior notice

Conduct Conditions Scholarship recipients who are subject to a formal disciplinary sanction may have their scholarship suspended pending the outcome. Recipients found guilty of academic misconduct may have their scholarship permanently withdrawn

Repayment Scholarships withdrawn due to misconduct, misrepresentation, or voluntary withdrawal from the programme may require repayment of funds already disbursed. The university reserves the right to pursue legal action to recover scholarship funds

Non-Transferable Scholarships are personal to the recipient and cannot be transferred, deferred beyond one academic year, or converted to cash. Requests to transfer scholarship funds to another person will be refused
False Application Students who provide false information in scholarship applications will have the award withdrawn and may be subject to academic misconduct proceedings and repayment demands

The Honor Code is an undertaking of the Stanford academic community, individually and collectively
Its purpose is to uphold a culture of academic honesty

Students will support this culture of academic honesty by neither giving nor accepting unpermitted academic aid in any work that serves as a component of grading or evaluation, including assignments, examinations, and research

Instructors will support this culture of academic honesty by providing clear guidance, both in their course syllabi and in response to student questions, on what constitutes permitted and unpermitted aid
Instructors will also not take unusual or unreasonable precautions to prevent academic dishonesty
Students and instructors will also cultivate an environment conducive to academic integrity

While instructors alone set academic requirements, the Honor Code is a community undertaking that requires students and instructors to work together to ensure conditions that support academic integrity
The Honor Code below applies to alleged incidents occurring on or after September 1, 2023. For cases filed before September 1, please see the previous Honor Code
Speak up if you're unsure whether or not conduct would violate these principles
Examples of conduct that have been regarded as being in violation of the Honor Code include
Copying from another s examination paper or allowing another to copy from one s own paper Unpermitted collaboration

Revising and resubmitting a quiz or exam for regrading, without the instructor s knowledge and consent Giving or receiving unpermitted aid on a take-home examination Representing as one s own work the work of another Giving or receiving aid on an academic assignment under circumstances in which a reasonable person should have known that such aid was not permitted
If asked what constitutes a violation of the Honor Code, most students might say "copying," "cheating" or "plagiarism."
As examples, they may think of someone purchasing a paper and submitting it for class, or looking over someone s shoulder during an exam
They may seem perfectly OK to the person committing violation
Example 1 Computer Science: Most people know that you are not allowed to look over someone s shoulder during an exam and use their answers as your own
Suppose, though, that you are writing code for a computer science project, and use code from a website for a minor function that is not crucial to the main goal of the assignment
This is still a violation of the Honor Code, because you are submitting the work as your own, rather than writing the code yourself
While it s true that in the real world, reusing code, particularly if it is efficient, is desirable, while you are in class, you are being graded on what you can write yourself
Not citing concepts, premises and ideas is plagiarism just as much as failing to cite specific text
Example 1 Take-home Exam: Generally, the rules of take-home exams state that you can t share your answers with others in the class
However, you are also not allowed to create outlines together or "prepare" your answers in some other way once the exam has been distributed

Example 2 Lab Class: In laboratory experiment courses a student "submitting work (including work on protocols, problem sets, and lab reports) that is identical or very similar to his/her partner's work, either in organization or structure, is a violation of the Honor Code
Specifically, this means that sharing of any written material is a violation."
You may have a lab partner in such classes, and work on the experiments together
Isn t it natural in those cases to share information for the lab reports?
Not if you are responsible for submitting lab reports individually
Example 3 Computer Science: You have a bug in the program you are writing, and can t find it
She may suggest strategies for debugging the code, but she may not look at your code for you and tell you where the bug is not even if you sit together and look for the bug
Again, you and you alone are responsible for the work you submit in class
While you may collaborate with others when you write code at an internship or elsewhere, doing so for coursework is a violation of the Honor Code
Example The Humanities: You wrote a paper last year for a History class that would be perfect, with some minor changes, for the Feminist Studies class you are taking this quarter
You did all the work yourself, properly cited everything it should be OK, right?
No, submitting work more than once is a violation of the Honor Code unless you have sought and been granted your professor s approval
Academic Integrity Working Group and Multi-Year Proctoring Study There are currently many concerns about academic dishonesty at Stanford

In order to formulate policies that will effectively address these concerns, it is necessary to identify the root causes (e.g., academic pressures, student mental health, etc) and scope of the problem as it truly stands

To that end, the university is providing the institutional mandate and resources (including access to all data deemed necessary) for a multi-year Academic Integrity Working Group ( AIWG ) to fully investigate this matter beginning in Fall 2023

The AIWG will also carry out a multi-year study beginning within the 2023-24 academic year (between two to four years in duration, to be determined by the AIWG) of equitable in-person proctoring* practices to answer student questions during exams and promote academic integrity by supervising the assessment process
The only proctoring permitted during this time will be that done under the auspices of the study
Remote proctoring, whether by software or humans through a computer, is not under consideration

The adoption of any policy on proctoring proposed by the AIWG after the conclusion of the study must be as new university policy approved by the Board on Conduct Affairs, the Undergraduate Senate of the Associated Students of Stanford University, the Graduate Student Council of the Associated Students of Stanford University, the Senate of the Academic Council, and the Office of the President
The study will address concerns about proctor presence and concerns about its absence in view of Stanford s present academic culture
It will assess the viability of in-person proctoring of exams to reduce cheating and to determine the impact on students taking exams (such as stress)
The resulting data will be the basis for future policy proposals by the AIWG to be voted upon by the same groups as at the present
The study will be led jointly by the AIWG and a disinterested, unbiased external consulting group with extensive experience working with student conduct, campus climate, and DEI concerns
The use of the external consulting group ensures proper and timely data collection, professional management of the data, and continuity regardless of turnover in the AIWG
The precise parameters governing the creation and charge of this study will be determined by both the AIWG and the external consulting group

The AIWG will advise Stanford administrators and governance bodies on any changes that need to be implemented in relation to Stanford s proctoring policy within one year after the conclusion of the study

It will a) apprise faculty and students university-wide of the study s results and its implications, and b) seek out stakeholder votes as needed for the timely implementation of the data-backed proposed changes
*The Honor Code text is intentionally silent on the topic of proctoring, subject to the constraints described here

Here, proctoring is defined as the reasonable supervision of exams by an exam administrator, and "in-person" includes traveling athletes, SCPD students, and other enrolled students taking an exam off campus

Composition and Operation The AIWG will consist of four students (graduate and undergraduate, including first-gen/low-income representation) and four faculty/lecturers (especially from departments with the most frequent instances of cheating concerns), along with one person each from the Office of Community Standards ( OCS ) and Office of General Counsel ( OGC )
Membership in the AIWG is to be determined by the ASSU Nominations Commission, the Faculty Senate Committee on Committees, and the VPSA (for OCS and OGC)
It will meet with stakeholders (student groups, departmental representatives, Academic Advising, etc.) to inform its proposals
The charge for the AIWG is to be set by the same university entities that set the charge for the Committee of Twelve

Grounds for Appeal Students may appeal assessment decisions on the following grounds only: (a) Procedural irregularity in the conduct of the assessment (b) Circumstances affecting performance that were not disclosed before the assessment (c) Administrative error in recording or calculating the result
Disagreement with the academic judgment of examiners is not grounds for appeal
Appeal Deadlines Appeals must be submitted within 20 working days of the release of results. Late appeals will not be considered except in exceptional documented circumstances

Frivolous Appeals Students submitting appeals found to be frivolous, vexatious, or without foundation may receive a formal warning. Repeat frivolous appeals may result in restrictions on future appeal rights

Academic Misconduct Appeals Appeals against academic misconduct findings follow a separate process. The standard of proof is balance of probabilities. The Appeals Panel decision is final. No further internal appeal is available after the Panel has ruled

Exam Irregularities Disruption of examinations including talking, use of unauthorized devices, or leaving without permission is subject to immediate removal and potential disqualification from the assessment

Section 1 Prohibited Conduct The following conduct is strictly prohibited and will result in formal disciplinary action: (a) Physical violence or threats of violence against any university community member (b) Harassment, bullying, or discriminatory behaviour on any protected ground (c) Unauthorized access to university IT systems or restricted facilities (d) Theft or wilful damage to university property or property of others (e) Possession, use, or distribution of illegal substances on campus (f) Forgery, falsification, or misrepresentation in academic or administrative matters
Section 2 Sanctions Students found in violation of this Code may face

Section 3 Appeals Process Students may appeal disciplinary decisions within 14 working days of notification. Appeals must be submitted in writing with supporting documentation. The Appeals Committee's decision is final and binding. No further internal appeal is available

Section 4 Substance Misuse Possession or distribution of illegal substances on campus is a critical violation. First-time offenders face mandatory counselling referral and probationary period. Repeat offenders are subject to immediate suspension pending investigation and possible expulsion

Data Collection The university collects personal data from students for purposes of admission, enrollment, assessment, and student welfare. Collection of data beyond these stated purposes requires explicit student consent

Access to Records Students have the right to access their personal academic records upon request. Requests must be processed within 30 days. Denial of access without lawful basis constitutes a breach of data protection legislation

Transcript Release Official transcripts are released only upon written student request or pursuant to a court order. Release of transcripts to third parties without student consent is prohibited except where required by law
Data Retention Student records are retained for a period of 6 years after the end of the student relationship. Records retained beyond this period without legal basis must be deleted upon request

Data Breach Any breach of student personal data must be reported to the Data Protection Officer within 24 hours of discovery. Failure to report a breach is a serious disciplinary offence and may constitute a criminal offence under applicable data protection law

Mandatory Engagement Students studying on a student visa must maintain engagement as required by immigration regulations. The university is required to report non-engaging students to the Home Office. Failure to engage may result in visa curtailment

Attendance Threshold A minimum attendance of 80% is required for all visa-holding students. Students falling below this threshold will receive a formal warning. Continued non-attendance results in mandatory reporting to immigration authorities
Unauthorized Absence Absence of more than 10 consecutive days without notification to the international student office constitutes unauthorized absence and triggers mandatory immigration reporting

Deferral and Interruption Students wishing to defer or interrupt their studies must notify the international student office immediately. Failure to update immigration records during a period of interruption may have serious consequences for current and future visa applications

End of Studies Students must leave the country by the visa expiry date following completion of studies. The university is required by law to report students who remain in the country beyond their authorized study period
As a student of the University, you are responsible for taking part in your education in an honest and authentic manner

Academic integrity is a core value of the University of Sydney, in both education and research. By joining the University community, you have agreed to uphold this value and to proactively contribute to the University s culture of academic integrity
Acting with academic integrity means doing the right thing even when things seem difficult and taking a shortcut to academic success seems easier
There is no such thing as achieving genuine academic success without first putting in hard work and effort
Behaving with academic integrity also means that you must acknowledge where other people, technologies or tools have contributed to your work in some way
This could include acknowledging words, ideas, images, computer code, or artistic or musical creations
It can also mean acknowledging any assistance provided by other people in relation to your work, including any feedback, proof-reading or editing
Acting with academic honesty also means you do not try to benefit academically by misleading others or being unfair to others, including gaining any kind of unfair advantage over others
When you act with academic honesty and integrity at university, having applied the proper effort, you can be proud that you have truly earned your qualification
You can be proud that you have developed your mind and skills to the tertiary level and be confident in your ability to succeed in your career
We expect you to be familiar with the policies and codes covering academic honesty and conduct at the University
The former policy and associated procedures, which were superseded in February 2023, are the Academic Honesty in Coursework Policy 2015 and the Academic Honesty Procedures 2016
These will apply for any breaches related to submissions prior to 20 February 2023, and as such you will be provided access to copies of them when relevant
The Academic Honesty Education Module (AHEM) is mandatory for all commencing coursework students. It takes about an hour to complete
You need to complete it by the census date of your first semester. Your academic record can be suppressed if it isn't completed
It will set you up with knowledge and support services so you can study with academic integrity
If you have any problems accessing or completing the module, contact the Office of Educational Integrity (OEI) at educational.integrity@sydney.edu.au
Completing the AHEM means you won't have to retake it for up to five years, even if you change courses
You will be automatically enrolled when you begin your course, and you should see it in your Canvas dashboard
Your unit of study outline will state if you need to complete the AHEM as a continuing student
You can complete or revisit the module voluntarily at any time during your studies
The AHEM is regularly reviewed with new information, especially about new technologies such as AI

At university there is a different style of learning with less focus on memorising knowledge delivered by teachers and more focus on locating and interpreting knowledge, and developing critical thinking skills

While this can be an adjustment at first, university is designed this way intentionally. If you are ever struggling, remember that there are a wide range of workshops and online resources to assist you with your studies
For example, the Using evidence and Avoiding Plagiarism modules helps you practice strategies to avoid plagiarism and identify the differences between quoting, paraphrasing and summarising

When you are being assessed for a group assignment, you need to contribute your own original work to the group and properly acknowledge any outside sources, tools or technologies used in the preparation of your work
If you don t, you put everyone in the group at risk of being accused of an academic integrity breach
Acting with academic honesty in group work also means that you commit fully to participating in group discussions and meeting agreed deadlines
It is unfair to leave your group members feeling like they need to complete work you were supposed to complete
It is even more unfair (and dishonest) to accept marks for group work if you have let others do your work for you

Examiner Appointment External examiners must be independent of the candidate and their supervisors. Appointment of examiners with a conflict of interest constitutes a procedural irregularity that may invalidate the examination outcome

Submission Standards Theses must conform to the prescribed format and word limits. Submissions that fail to meet formatting requirements may be returned unchecked. Repeated failure to comply with submission standards may result in the thesis being deemed not ready for examination

Viva Voce Examination The viva voce is a compulsory component of postgraduate research degrees. Failure to attend a scheduled viva without prior written notice and valid cause results in automatic failure. A second viva attempt is at the discretion of the Examination Board

Examination Outcomes Possible outcomes are: pass without corrections, minor corrections (within 3 months), major corrections (within 12 months), resubmission (within 18 months), or fail with no resubmission. A fail outcome cannot be appealed on grounds of academic judgment

Plagiarism in Research Degrees Plagiarism detected in a submitted thesis after award results in revocation of the degree. The university reserves the right to revoke awards up to 6 years after conferral upon discovery of academic misconduct
What is Academic Integrity, why is it important, and what happens if you breach it?
Academic Integrity means being honest in your academic work, and making sure that you formally recognise and reference the existing knowledge and ideas on which your work is based

If you do not acknowledge the work or ideas of others, you could be penalised for Academic Misconduct, which is defined as any action or attempted action that may result in you obtaining an unfair academic advantage
