Prohibition The company has a zero-tolerance approach to bribery and corruption. Offering, promising, giving, requesting, or accepting a bribe in any form is strictly prohibited and constitutes gross misconduct subject to immediate dismissal and referral to law enforcement authorities

Gifts and Hospitality Gifts with a value exceeding 50 / $50 must be declared to the compliance team. Acceptance of gifts from suppliers or clients above this threshold without declaration constitutes a breach of this policy

Facilitation Payments Facilitation payments small payments made to officials to speed up routine processes are prohibited regardless of local practice. Any demand for a facilitation payment must be reported to the compliance team immediately

Due Diligence The company conducts due diligence on third parties before entering commercial relationships. Employees who engage third parties without required due diligence approval may be held personally liable for resulting regulatory penalties

Reporting Employees who become aware of actual or suspected bribery must report it immediately via the confidential whistleblowing channel. Failure to report known bribery may itself constitute a criminal offence

Attendance and Punctuality Employees must report to work on time and maintain regular attendance. Three unexcused absences within 30 days constitute grounds for immediate dismissal. Excessive tardiness after a written warning may result in termination

Confidentiality Employees must not disclose confidential company information to unauthorized parties during or after employment. Violations result in immediate termination and may subject the employee to civil litigation and claims for damages

Workplace Harassment Harassment of any kind is strictly prohibited. Employees found guilty of harassment face suspension without pay or termination. Serious cases are referred directly to law enforcement without prior warning
Company Resources Company equipment must be used for business purposes only. Misuse or theft results in immediate termination and potential criminal prosecution

Conflict of Interest Employees must disclose conflicts of interest immediately. Undisclosed conflicts including financial relationships with competitors result in termination for cause and possible civil action
Social Media Employees must not post confidential or defamatory content online. Violations may result in disciplinary action, termination, and legal proceedings

Professional Standards All workplace communications including email, messaging platforms, and written correspondence must be professional, respectful, and free from offensive content. Communications that constitute harassment, discrimination, or bullying are gross misconduct offences

Email Use Company email accounts must be used for business purposes only. Personal use is permitted on a minimal basis but must not involve inappropriate, illegal, or offensive content. The company reserves the right to monitor email accounts on company systems

Confidentiality in Communications Employees must not include confidential company information in external emails without encryption or explicit authorisation. Inadvertent disclosure of confidential information through email must be reported to the IT department immediately

Legal Holds Where litigation is anticipated, employees must preserve all relevant communications and must not delete emails or messages related to the matter. Destruction of potentially relevant communications constitutes spoliation and may result in serious legal consequences

Record Keeping Employees in regulated roles must ensure that material communications are recorded in accordance with applicable regulatory requirements. Failure to maintain required records is a regulatory offence and a disciplinary matter

Early Resolution Line managers are expected to address interpersonal conflicts at the earliest opportunity. Failure to address known conflicts that escalate into formal grievances or tribunal claims may be considered a management performance issue

Mediation The company may offer mediation as an alternative to formal grievance procedures. Participation in mediation is voluntary. Refusal to engage in offered mediation is not penalised but will be noted if the matter proceeds to formal proceedings

Formal Grievance Where early resolution fails, employees may raise a formal grievance. The formal grievance procedure must be followed before an employment tribunal claim can be brought in most circumstances
Vexatious Conduct Employees who repeatedly raise complaints found to be without merit or who pursue disputes for improper purposes may be subject to disciplinary action for vexatious behaviour

Non-Retaliation No employee will suffer detriment for raising a genuine workplace conflict or participating in conflict resolution procedures. Retaliation constitutes gross misconduct subject to disciplinary action

Acceptable Use Company IT systems are for business use only. Personal use beyond minimal incidental use is prohibited. Accessing inappropriate, illegal, or offensive content on company systems is a gross misconduct offence
Password Security Employees must not share login credentials under any circumstances. Sharing passwords resulting in a security breach is treated as gross misconduct subject to immediate dismissal

Data Handling Customer and company data must be handled in accordance with data protection laws. Unauthorized disclosure, transfer, or loss of personal data is a serious disciplinary offence and may constitute a criminal offence
Monitoring The company reserves the right to monitor use of company IT systems. Employees have no expectation of privacy when using company equipment or networks
Software Installation Installation of unauthorized software on company devices is prohibited. Violations may result in disciplinary action and the employee may be charged for remediation costs

Progressive Discipline Standard disciplinary process: Step 1: Verbal warning (documented in writing) Step 2: Written warning Step 3: Final written warning or suspension without pay (up to 5 days) Step 4: Termination of employment

Gross Misconduct Gross misconduct results in immediate dismissal without notice or payment in lieu. Examples include: assault, intoxication at work, fraud, deliberate destruction of property, or serious confidentiality breaches
Performance Management Employees failing to meet defined performance targets after a documented Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) of 60 days may be dismissed for incapability
Appeal Rights Employees may appeal any disciplinary decision within 10 working days of written notification. The HR Director's decision on appeal is final

Prohibition Employees must not be under the influence of alcohol or illegal drugs while at work, on company premises, or while operating company vehicles. Attendance at work while impaired is gross misconduct subject to immediate dismissal

Testing The company reserves the right to conduct drug and alcohol testing where there is reasonable cause or following a workplace accident. Refusal to submit to testing without a valid medical reason is treated as a positive result and may result in dismissal

Prescription Medication Employees taking prescription medication that may affect their performance or ability to operate machinery must notify occupational health. Failure to disclose relevant medication that subsequently causes an incident may be treated as a disciplinary matter
Social Events Excessive alcohol consumption at company social events that results in misconduct is subject to the same disciplinary procedures as misconduct at the workplace

Support The company encourages employees with substance misuse issues to seek support. Self-referral to the employee assistance programme will be treated confidentially and will not automatically trigger disciplinary action

Commitment The organisation is committed to equality of opportunity for all employees and applicants. Discrimination, harassment, or victimisation on any protected ground is unlawful and constitutes gross misconduct

Protected Characteristics The following characteristics are protected: age, disability, gender reassignment, marriage and civil partnership, pregnancy and maternity, race, religion or belief, sex, and sexual orientation. Any adverse treatment connected to these characteristics is prohibited

Harassment Harassment related to a protected characteristic is prohibited. This includes unwanted conduct that has the purpose or effect of violating dignity or creating an intimidating, hostile, degrading, humiliating, or offensive environment. Perpetrators face disciplinary action up to and including dismissal
Reporting Employees who experience or witness discrimination or harassment must report it to HR. Victimisation of employees who report in good faith is a serious disciplinary offence
Third Parties Harassment by clients, customers, or contractors must be reported to management. The organisation will take reasonable steps to protect employees from third-party harassment

Eligible Expenses Only expenses that are wholly, exclusively, and necessarily incurred in the performance of employment duties are reimbursable. Personal expenses will not be reimbursed under any circumstances

Approval Requirement All expenses above 50 / $50 require prior manager approval. Submitting expenses without prior approval may result in rejection and repeated violations will be treated as a disciplinary matter

Receipts Original receipts must accompany all expense claims. Claims without receipts will not be processed. Submission of falsified or altered receipts constitutes fraud and is subject to immediate dismissal and potential criminal prosecution

Travel Business travel must use the most economical option unless operational reasons require otherwise. First-class travel requires Director-level approval. Unauthorised first-class claims will be reimbursed at standard class rates only

Expense Fraud Submitting false, duplicate, or inflated expense claims constitutes gross misconduct and fraud. The organisation will seek recovery of fraudulently claimed amounts and may pursue criminal prosecution
Eligibility All employees with 26 weeks continuous service have the statutory right to request flexible working. The company will consider all requests in a reasonable manner

Request Process Requests must be submitted in writing and must explain the proposed working pattern and the effect on the business. The company will respond within 3 months of receiving a request. Failure to respond within the statutory timeframe entitles the employee to make a tribunal complaint
Grounds for Refusal Requests may be refused on specific statutory grounds only. Refusal on grounds related to a protected characteristic constitutes indirect discrimination

Trial Periods The company may offer a trial period for a proposed flexible working arrangement. Failure to comply with agreed conditions during a trial period may result in termination of the arrangement

Appeal Employees may appeal a refusal within 10 working days. The appeal outcome is final within the internal process. Employees retain the right to bring a tribunal claim where the process has not been followed correctly

Right to Raise a Grievance All employees have the right to raise a grievance about their work, working conditions, or treatment by colleagues or management without fear of retaliation. Victimisation of employees who raise grievances constitutes gross misconduct

Informal Resolution Employees are encouraged to resolve grievances informally with their line manager in the first instance. Where informal resolution is not appropriate or has failed, a formal grievance may be submitted

Formal Grievance Procedure Formal grievances must be submitted in writing to HR. A grievance hearing will be arranged within 10 working days. The employee has the right to be accompanied by a trade union representative or colleague

Outcome and Appeal The outcome of a grievance investigation will be communicated in writing within 5 working days of the hearing. Employees may appeal the outcome within 10 working days. The appeal decision is final
Malicious Complaints Employees who submit grievances found to be deliberately false or malicious may be subject to disciplinary action

Employer Obligations The organisation is committed to providing a safe working environment. Failure to maintain required safety standards may result in regulatory enforcement action, fines, and legal liability

Employee Responsibilities Employees must comply with all health and safety procedures. Failure to follow safety procedures, tampering with safety equipment, or creating hazardous conditions constitutes gross misconduct subject to immediate dismissal

Incident Reporting All workplace accidents, near-misses, and hazards must be reported immediately. Failure to report incidents is a disciplinary offence. Falsifying incident reports constitutes gross misconduct

Risk Assessments Managers must ensure risk assessments are completed and reviewed annually. Conducting high-risk activities without a valid risk assessment is prohibited and may result in regulatory prosecution

Alcohol and Drugs Attending work under the influence of alcohol or non-prescribed drugs is a gross misconduct offence subject to immediate dismissal. The organisation reserves the right to require drug and alcohol testing where there is reasonable suspicion

Ownership All intellectual property created by employees in the course of their employment belongs to the company. This includes inventions, software, designs, written works, and trade secrets created using company resources or during working hours

Disclosure Employees must disclose all potentially patentable inventions or commercially valuable intellectual property to the company immediately upon creation. Failure to disclose may result in disciplinary action and legal proceedings

Personal Projects Employees wishing to develop intellectual property in their own time must obtain prior written clearance where the work is related to the company's field of business. Failure to obtain clearance may result in the company asserting ownership

Third-Party IP Employees must not incorporate third-party intellectual property into company work without appropriate licensing. Infringement of third-party IP rights exposes the company to legal liability and may result in disciplinary action against the responsible employee

Post-Employment Obligations regarding protection of company intellectual property and trade secrets continue after termination of employment. Misuse of company IP after departure may result in injunctive relief and claims for damages

Annual Leave Employees are entitled to 20 days paid annual leave per year. Unused leave must be taken within the calendar year. Carry-forward of more than 5 days requires written management approval and is not guaranteed

Sick Leave Employees receive 10 days paid sick leave per year. Medical certification is required for absences exceeding 3 consecutive days. Misuse of sick leave constitutes misconduct subject to disciplinary action including dismissal
Unauthorized Absence Absence for 3 or more consecutive days without notification constitutes abandonment of employment and may result in automatic termination of contract

Leave During Notice Period Annual leave cannot be taken during the notice period without explicit written HR approval. Unauthorized leave during notice is deducted from final salary and may result in legal action to recover losses

Maternity Leave Entitlement All employees are entitled to 52 weeks of maternity leave regardless of length of service. Statutory Maternity Pay is payable for 39 weeks subject to eligibility criteria. Denial of maternity leave rights constitutes unlawful discrimination

Notification Requirements Employees must notify HR of pregnancy and intended maternity leave dates at least 15 weeks before the expected week of childbirth. Failure to notify does not remove entitlement but may affect pay arrangements

Keeping in Touch Days Employees may work up to 10 Keeping in Touch days during maternity leave without losing their entitlement. Compulsion to work during maternity leave beyond KIT days constitutes a breach of statutory rights

Return to Work Employees returning from maternity leave have the right to return to the same job. Where this is not reasonably practicable, a suitable alternative must be offered. Failure to reinstate on return constitutes automatic unfair dismissal

Paternity Leave Eligible employees are entitled to 2 weeks of paid paternity leave to be taken within 56 days of the child's birth. Requests for paternity leave must not be refused where the employee meets eligibility criteria

Annual Reviews All employees receive a formal performance review annually. Failure by managers to complete required performance reviews constitutes a management conduct issue subject to HR intervention

Performance Improvement Plan Employees whose performance falls below the required standard will be placed on a formal Performance Improvement Plan (PIP) of 60 to 90 days. Failure to achieve PIP targets may result in demotion or termination of employment

Rating Process Performance ratings must be based on objective evidence. Discrimination, bias, or personal relationships must not influence ratings. Managers found to have manipulated ratings are subject to disciplinary action

Underperformance Persistent underperformance after a PIP without improvement constitutes grounds for dismissal for capability. The standard disciplinary process does not apply to capability dismissals
Retaliation Retaliating against an employee for raising concerns about the performance review process is prohibited and constitutes gross misconduct
Duration All new employees serve a probationary period of 6 months. The probationary period may be extended by up to 3 months where performance or conduct concerns have been identified

Performance Standards Employees must demonstrate satisfactory performance, attendance, and conduct during probation. Failure to meet required standards during probation may result in termination of employment without following the full disciplinary procedure
Review Meetings Formal probationary reviews are conducted at 3 and 6 months. Failure to attend a scheduled probationary review without prior notice may be treated as a conduct issue

Notice During Probation The notice period during probation is one week on either side. The company reserves the right to terminate employment during probation without following the standard disciplinary procedure where performance is unsatisfactory
Confirmation of Employment Confirmation of employment is not automatic. Written confirmation must be received before the employee is considered to have passed probation

Equal Opportunity All recruitment must comply with equal opportunity legislation. Discrimination on grounds of age, sex, race, disability, religion, or sexual orientation in any stage of recruitment is prohibited and may result in legal action against the organisation and the individual responsible

Job Advertising All vacancies must be advertised through approved channels. Appointing candidates without following the approved recruitment process constitutes a policy violation subject to disciplinary action

Pre-Employment Checks All offers of employment are conditional on satisfactory completion of reference checks, right-to-work verification, and where applicable, criminal record checks. Offers made without completing required checks may be withdrawn

Misrepresentation Candidates who provide false information during recruitment will have offers withdrawn or employment terminated if discovered post-hire. Legal action may be pursued for serious misrepresentation
Internal Applications Internal applicants must inform their line manager before applying for internal vacancies. Failure to disclose an internal application may result in withdrawal of the candidacy

Selection Criteria Redundancy selection must be based on objective, non-discriminatory criteria such as skills, performance, and attendance. Selection based on protected characteristics constitutes unlawful discrimination and exposes the organisation to tribunal claims

Consultation A minimum consultation period of 30 days is required for 20 or more redundancies. Failure to consult constitutes a breach of statutory obligations and may result in a protective award of up to 90 days' pay per affected employee

Redundancy Pay Eligible employees are entitled to statutory redundancy pay calculated by age and length of service. Withholding lawful redundancy pay is unlawful and may result in employment tribunal proceedings

Alternative Employment The company must consider and offer suitable alternative employment where it exists. Unreasonable refusal of a suitable alternative offer by an employee may disqualify them from statutory redundancy pay

Appeal Employees have the right to appeal redundancy selection within 10 working days. Failure to provide an appeal process constitutes a breach of the ACAS Code of Practice and may increase tribunal awards

Approved Equipment Employees must only use company-approved devices for remote work. Use of personal devices to access company systems without IT approval is prohibited and constitutes a data security violation subject to disciplinary action

Network Security Employees must connect to company systems via the approved VPN at all times. Accessing company data on unsecured public Wi-Fi networks is prohibited. Violations that result in data breaches may result in dismissal and financial liability

Physical Security Employees must ensure that screens are not visible to unauthorised persons. Printed documents containing company data must be securely destroyed and must not be left accessible to household members

Incident Reporting Any suspected security incident including lost devices, phishing attempts, or unauthorised access must be reported to IT Security within 1 hour of discovery. Failure to report incidents promptly is a disciplinary offence

Home Environment The company reserves the right to conduct virtual security assessments of home working environments. Employees must comply with reasonable security requirements or risk losing remote working privileges

Personal Use Employees may use personal social media outside working hours but must not post content that brings the organisation into disrepute, discloses confidential information, or constitutes harassment of colleagues, clients, or competitors. Violations may result in disciplinary action up to and including dismissal

Company Accounts Only authorised employees may post on official company social media accounts. Unauthorised posting on company accounts constitutes a serious breach of policy subject to disciplinary action

Confidentiality Employees must never disclose commercially sensitive, confidential, or personal data through social media. Such disclosures may constitute gross misconduct and a breach of data protection law
Monitoring The company reserves the right to monitor publicly available social media content of employees where there is reasonable cause related to conduct or performance

Notice Periods Employees must provide the notice period stated in their contract. Failure to serve the required notice period entitles the company to seek damages for losses incurred. Payment in lieu of notice may be offered at the company's discretion

Garden Leave The company may place employees on garden leave during the notice period. Employees on garden leave remain bound by all contractual obligations including confidentiality and non-compete clauses. Breach of obligations during garden leave may result in legal action

Exit Interviews Exit interviews are requested for all departing employees. While attendance is not mandatory, refusal to participate will be noted. Information shared in exit interviews is confidential and will not be used in disciplinary proceedings

Return of Property All company property including devices, access cards, and documents must be returned on or before the last day of employment. Failure to return property within 5 working days entitles the company to deduct replacement costs from the final salary

Post-Employment Restrictions Employees in senior or commercial roles may be subject to post-employment restrictive covenants. Breach of non-compete or non-solicitation clauses may result in injunctive relief and claims for damages

Mandatory Training Employees must complete all mandatory training within the prescribed timescales. Failure to complete mandatory compliance training (including data protection, health and safety, and anti-bribery) without a valid reason is a disciplinary offence

Sponsored Study Employees receiving financial support for external qualifications must remain with the company for a minimum period following completion. Resignation before the required period results in a proportional repayment of training costs

Training Agreements Employees receiving substantial training investment may be required to sign a training agreement. Breach of a signed training agreement entitles the company to recover training costs through deduction from salary or legal proceedings

Time Off for Study Eligible employees have the statutory right to request reasonable time off for study or training. This right applies to employees with 26 weeks continuous service. Unreasonable refusal may give rise to a tribunal complaint

Qualification Misrepresentation Employees who misrepresent their qualifications or training records will be subject to disciplinary action including dismissal. Misrepresentation discovered after employment commences may result in termination for fraud

Protection Employees who make protected disclosures in good faith are protected from dismissal, demotion, or any other detriment. Retaliation against a whistleblower constitutes gross misconduct and may give rise to legal liability

Reporting Channels Concerns may be raised with the line manager, HR Director, or the designated confidential whistleblowing hotline. Reports may be made anonymously, though this may limit the ability to investigate fully
Malicious Disclosures Employees who make disclosures known to be false or made in bad faith are not protected and may be subject to disciplinary action
Confidentiality The identity of the whistleblower will be kept confidential to the extent possible. Breach of whistleblower confidentiality by any staff member is a serious disciplinary offence

Appendix B: Subject Matter Expert (SME) Workshops List of Participating Agencies
OPM provides policy guidance and advice to agencies governmentwide on such topics as taking performance-based actions and adverse actions
In addition, we provide guidance on other topics such as alternative dispute resolution , reasonable accommodations , and appeal rights to include employee coverage and affirmative defenses
Most Federal agencies are authorized under 5 U.S.C. chapter 75 to suspend, demote, furlough, or remove employees for "such cause as will promote the efficiency of the service."
Actions taken under 5 U.S.C. chapter 75 are "adverse actions" and are based upon misconduct, unacceptable performance, or a combination of both
They may also be based upon non-disciplinary reasons such as medical inability to perform or furlough
We issue governmentwide regulations at 5 CFR part 752 which implement the law
Most Federal agencies are authorized under 5 U.S.C. chapter 43 to demote, or remove employees for "unacceptable performance."
Actions based on unacceptable performance may also be taken under 5 U.S.C. chapter 75 and are regulated at 5 CFR part 752
Alternative Dispute Resolution (ADR) is a procedure for settling disputes by means other than litigation
Often times, a third-party neutral assists in reaching an amicable resolution through the use of various techniques
ADR contributes to effective management by controlling the costs of conflict, producing quicker and more durable results, and preserving resources for the mission of the agency
The alternative methods to traditional, formal dispute resolution include mediation, fact-finding, ombudsing, interest-based negotiation, arbitration, and alternative discipline
Alternative methods involve the use of alternatives to traditional disciplinary procedures and penalties
Through the effective use of ADR agencies resolve employee-employer disputes in a way that is more efficient and more effective than traditional, adversarial methods of dispute resolution
Federal employees have a variety of appeal and grievance rights

Depending on the issues involved, they may pursue the matter within their agency, appeal to the Merit Systems Protection Board (MSPB) or file a complaint with the Equal Employment Opportunity Commission (EEOC) or the Office of Special Counsel (OSC)
Employees generally have the right to appeal a suspension, demotion, or removal to the MSPB or to grieve the action through the agency's negotiated grievance procedure
Employees can choose between these two methods of appeal, but cannot pursue both avenues
Allegations of discrimination, reprisal for whistleblowing, and other prohibited personnel practices can be raised as part of an employee's appeal or grievance
Such allegations can also be filed directly with the agency's Equal Employment Office or the OSC
Employees working during their probationary/trial periods generally are not covered under 5 CFR parts 432 or 752 of the Code of Federal Regulations

Except for certain circumstances , if an action is warranted against a probationer, he or she can appeal the termination to the Merit Systems Protection Board only if the action is based on marital status or partisan political affiliation

This is due, in part, to the very nature of the probationary/trial period which provides supervisors the opportunity to determine whether a new employee will be an asset rather than a liability to the organization
An individual must receive full procedural and appeal rights if he or she meets the definition of "employee" provided at
Employees working during their probationary/trial periods generally are not covered
In certain circumstances, however, individuals working in probationary/trial periods have prior current continuous service that may qualify them to receive full procedural and appeal rights
As a result, it is advisable to always review the requirements at chapter 75
Supervisors should consult with their servicing human resources office to help them determine if an individual meets the statutory definition of "employee."
An individual must receive full procedural and appeal rights if he or she is
in the competitive service and has completed one year of current continuous service under other than a temporary appointment limited to one year or less

a preference eligible in the excepted service who has completed one year of current continuous service in the same or similar positions in an Executive agency; or in the United States Postal Service or Postal Regulatory Commission; or

an individual in the excepted service (other than a preference eligible) who is not serving a probationary or trial period under an initial appointment pending conversion to the competitive service, for example

an individual in the excepted service (other than a preference eligible) who has completed two years of current continuous service in the same or similar positions in an Executive agency under other than a temporary appointment limited to two years or less

Employees may introduce evidence in response to and in defense against an agency action by providing evidence such as a new fact or set of facts to defeat actions taken against them, (e.g., an adverse action under 5 CFR part 752 or a performance-based action under 5 CFR part 432 ), even if the facts supporting the actions are true
Affirmative defenses include claims that the agency action was contrary to the law or that a harmful procedural error occurred

If the action is otherwise appealable to the Merit Systems Protection Board (MSPB), individuals can raise as an affirmative defense discrimination based on partisan political reasons, marital status, race, color, religion, sex, national origin, age (must be at least 40 years old) or handicapping condition
Individuals may also allege as an affirmative defense a prohibited personnel practice under
Probationary employees only have a right to raise certain affirmative defenses
An employee terminated during the probationary period may appeal to the Board on the grounds that the termination was based on partisan political reasons or marital status
An individual entitled to appeal on any of these grounds may also raise an allegation of prohibited discrimination
In certain circumstances , an individual serving a probationary period may have additional appeal rights
Disability discrimination, discrimination under Title VII, and whistleblower reprisal are some of the affirmative defenses that can be raised
Federal agencies are required by law to provide reasonable accommodation to qualified employees with disabilities
The U.S. Equal Opportunity Commission (EEOC) enforces the law which prohibits employment discrimination against qualified individuals with disabilities
EEOC also provides oversight and coordination of all federal equal employment opportunity regulations, practices, and policies
The Rehabilitation Act of 1973 Section 501 , as amended applies to employees in the federal sector and prohibits employment discrimination against individuals with disabilities
The Civil Service Reform Act of 1978 (CSRA) identifies prohibited personnel practices
The CSRA prohibits any employee who has authority to take certain personnel actions from discriminating for or against employees or applicants for employment on protected bases, including disability
The prohibited personnel practices and the merit system principles for Federal personnel management are codified at 5 U.S.C. 2302

The Director of the U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM) is authorized by law to seek reconsideration of erroneous decisions by the U.S. Merit Systems Protection Board (the Board) and arbitral awards involving appealable adverse actions

Under 5 U.S.C. 7703(d) , the Director may file petitions for reconsideration and judicial review of a Board final decision if the Director determines that: (1) the Board erred in interpreting a civil service law, rule, or regulation affecting personnel management; and (2) the Board s decision will have a substantial impact on a civil service law, rule, regulation, or policy directive

Section 7121(f) likewise authorizes the Director to seek reconsideration and judicial review of an arbitrator s award, provided that the arbitrator s decision involves matters covered under 5 U.S.C. 4303 or 7512 or similar matters which arise under other personnel systems

Further, as required by 5 CFR 1201.119(b) , OPM must file its petition for reconsideration to the Board (or arbitrator) within 35 days after the date of service of the final Board (or arbitral) decision
This 35-day deadline to file a petition for reconsideration gives OPM a limited period in which to conduct a full and careful assessment of the decision

If OPM s reconsideration petition is denied, and OPM wishes to seek judicial review, 5 U.S.C. 7703(d) requires that then OPM must file its petition for judicial review within 60 days from the issuance of the Board s final order or decision (or the arbitrator s decision) that denied its petition for reconsideration

OPM cannot seek judicial review unless it has timely sought reconsideration, or intervened in the case at an earlier stage, which makes meeting the regulatory deadline in 5 CFR 1201.119(b) to submit a petition for reconsideration crucial for any further action on a Board or arbitral decision

Due to these considerations, it is imperative that any agency interested in seeking reconsideration of a Board decision, or a qualifying arbitral decision, contact OPM s Accountability and Workforce Relations (AWR) team as quickly as possible after the agency receives the decision from the Board or the arbitrator
The U.S. Office of Personnel Management (OPM) provides leadership on Federal leave policies and programs

We accomplish this by developing and maintaining Governmentwide regulations and policies for agencies to use to administer leave, including annual leave, sick leave, the Family and Medical Leave Act, Federal leave sharing programs, military leave, and time off for special circumstances - e.g., early dismissal or closure for weather emergencies

Ultimately, each Federal agency is responsible for complying with the law and OPM's Governmentwide regulations and following OPM's policies and guidance to administer leave policies and programs for its own employees
On June 26, 2013, the Supreme Court ruled that Section 3 of the Defense of Marriage Act (DOMA) is unconstitutional

As a result of the Supreme Court s decision, the United States Office of Personnel Management (OPM) will now be able to extend certain benefits to Federal employees and annuitants who have legally married a spouse of the same sex, regardless of the employee s or annuitant s state of residency
OPM is currently in the process of updating and revising the website to reflect this change, and will be updating this information as soon as possible

This Collaboration Agreement (the "Agreement") is made as of April 14th, 2020 (the "Effective Date") by and between Anixa Biosciences, Inc., a Delaware corporation, located at 3150 Almaden Expressway, Suite 250, San Jose, CA 95118, U.S.A. ("Anixa"), and OntoChem GmbH, a German limited liability company, located at Bl cherstr
Anixa and OntoChem are referred to herein individually as a "Party" and collectively as the "Parties."
WHEREAS, the Parties wish to collaborate in the discovery and development of novel drug candidates for the treatment of COVID-19 in accordance with the terms and conditions of this Agreement

NOW, THEREFORE, in consideration of the premises and the mutual promises set forth in this Agreement, and other good and valuable consideration, the receipt and sufficiency of which are hereby acknowledged, the Parties agree as follows
1.1 "Affiliate" means, with respect to a Party, any entity directly or indirectly controlled by, controlling or under common control with such Party

For purposes of this definition, "control" means (a) ownership of fifty percent (50%) (or such lesser percentage which is the maximum allowed to be owned by a foreign entity or investor in a particular jurisdiction) or more of the outstanding voting stock or other ownership interest of an entity, or (b) possession of the power to (i) elect, appoint, direct or remove fifty percent (50%) or more of the members of the board of directors or other governing body of an entity or (ii) otherwise direct or cause the direction of the management or policies of an entity by contract or otherwise
1.2 "Hit Compound" means any chemical entity that is determined in performing the Research Plan to meet the Hit Criteria
1.3 "Hit Criteria" means the criteria identified as "Hit Criteria" as set forth in the Research Plan

1.4 "Invention" means any invention, know-how, data, discovery or proprietary information, whether or not patentable, that is made or generated solely by the Representatives of Anixa or OntoChem or jointly by the Representatives of Anixa and OntoChem in performing the Research Plan, including all intellectual property rights in the foregoing
1.5 "Representative" means, with respect to a Party, an officer, director, employee, agent or permitted subcontractor of such Party
1.6 "Research Plan" means the research plan attached hereto as Exhibit A

1.7 "SAR" means the relationship between the chemical or three-dimensional structure of a compound and its biological activity, and includes the determination of the chemical groups responsible for evoking a target biological effect

1.8 "Target" means: (a) any protease of any coronavirus, including Mpro; (b) the Nsp15-pRB ribonuclease protein- protein interaction; (c) all mutants and variants of any molecule or component referenced in clauses (a) or (b); and (d) all truncated forms (including fragments) of any molecule or component referenced in clauses (a) or (b) or mutant or variant referenced in clause (c)

1.9 "Variant" means, with respect to any Hit Compound: (a) all compounds within the genus of compounds to which such Hit Compound would belong under United States patent laws as referenced in the Selection Notice (as defined below); and (b) any base form, metabolite, ester, salt form, racemate, stereoisomer, polymorph, hydrate, anhydride or solvate of such Hit Compound or any other compound described in clause (a) (in the case of this clause (b), without regard to whether such compound is referenced in the Selection Notice)

The Parties will diligently perform their respective activities set forth in the Research Plan (such activities, collectively, the "Research Program") in accordance with the timelines set forth therein, with the objective of identifying Hit Compounds and Lead Scaffolds that modulate the applicable Target

Without limiting the foregoing, OntoChem will (a) provide all deliverables set forth in the Research Plan (each, a "Deliverable") and (b) obtain any authorizations, approvals and licenses required for performance of the Research Plan

If any terms set forth in the Research Plan conflict with the terms set forth in this Agreement, the terms of this Agreement will control unless expressly indicated to the contrary in the Research Plan
The Research Plan may not be amended without the prior written consent of both Parties

If, from time to time, the Parties desire to expand the scope of the Research Program, then they will negotiate in good faith a potential amendment of the Research Plan in regard to such expanded scope, on commercially reasonable terms, but neither Party will be obligated to enter into any such amendment

OntoChem will provide Anixa with weekly (or more frequently as requested) updates regarding its progress under the Research Program via teleconference, videoconference or e-mail, and the Parties will make appropriate personnel available in a timely manner to discuss and provide feedback in regard to such updates
In conjunction with each weekly update described in Section 2.2, OntoChem will deliver to Anixa all data generated under the Research Plan since the preceding update

In addition, Anixa will have the right to reasonably request additional information relating to such data, and OntoChem will respond to such requests promptly with any such additional information in its possession or control, provided that, for clarity, OntoChem will not be required to perform any new or additional research in order to generate any such additional information

Within one year following completion of all activities under the Research Plan (the "Selection Deadline"), Anixa, in good faith consultation with OntoChem, will have the right to select up to two hundred (200) Hit Compounds (each, a "Selected Hit Compound"), by providing OntoChem with written notice of such Selected Hit Compound(s)

(the "Selection Notice"), and each Selected Hit Compound, along with all Variants of such Selected Hit Compound referenced in the Selection Notice, is hereby designated as a "Lead Scaffold" under this Agreement

Commencing upon selection of a Selected Hit Compound, Anixa (itself and through its Affiliates and designees) will have sole authority over and control of the further development, manufacture, and commercialization of the corresponding Lead Scaffold and any product candidate or product incorporating a compound from such Lead Scaffold

Following the Selection Deadline, Anixa will have no further rights with respect to any Hit Compound that is not a Selected Hit Compound or included within a Lead Scaffold (each, a "Rejected Hit Compound"), provided that, during the period of two (2) years following the Selection Deadline, neither OntoChem nor any of its Affiliates will use or disclose to any third party any Rejected Hit Compound or any Variant thereof, including the identity, structure or SAR information of any such compound, for application as anti-viral agents or protease inhibitors, for purposes of modulating any Target or for treatment of virus-related conditions

In case OntoChem finds a novel and unexpected antiviral use of those Rejected Hit Compounds during this 2-years period, it will notify Anixa about these findings and Anixa has the right of first negotiation during a period of 6 months after this notification

If Anixa decides to not license those uses or compounds for this novel antiviral use, OntoChem is free to develop those molecules further as its own intellectual property without any further restrictions

OntoChem may engage one or more subcontractors to perform its activities under the Research Plan with the prior written approval of Anixa and provided that, with respect to any such subcontractor, OntoChem will (a) be responsible and liable for the performance of such subcontractor and (b) enter into a written agreement (i) consistent with terms and conditions of this Agreement, including with respect to confidentiality and intellectual property, and (ii) prohibiting such subcontractor from further subcontracting
For clarity, vendors where commercial building blocks or compounds will be purchased are nor regarded as subcontractors

During the term of this Agreement, except in the performance of its obligations or exercise of its rights under this Agreement, neither OntoChem nor any of its Affiliates will discover, research, develop, manufacture or commercialize any compound or product directed to any Target, either independently or for or in collaboration with a third party (including the grant of a license to any third party), or have any of the foregoing activities performed on behalf of OntoChem or any of its Affiliates by a third party
For clarity, the foregoing includes the screening (including via computational methods) of any compound library or virtual compound library against any Target

pay OntoChem 100,002 Euros in six (6) equal installments as follows: (i) 16,667 Euros within five (5) days after the Effective Date; and (ii) five (5) installments in the amount of 16,667 Euros on each one-month anniversary of the Effective Date, except that the last such payment will be due within thirty (30) days after completion of all activities under the Research Plan; and

1.1 "Maximum Daily Delivery Obligation (MDDO)" means the maximum daily quantity of natural gas, expressed in Dekatherms (Dth), that Transporter is obligated to deliver from time to time at the Point(s) of Delivery specified in Exhibit B to the executed Agreement

1.2 "Maximum Daily Quantity (MDQ) " means the maximum daily quantity of natural gas, expressed* in Dth's, that Transporter is obligated under the executed Agreement to transport on behalf of' Shipper, which shall be 23,000 Dth
1.3 "Equivalent Quantity" means the quantity, expressed in Dth's, delivered to Shipper by Transporter at the Point(s) of Delivery
Such quantity is equal to the quantity of gas received from Shipper at the Point(s) of Receipt less Fuel Usage and Applicable Shrinkage

1.4 "Fuel Usage and Applicable Shrinkage" means the quantity of natural gas retained by Transporter for fuel usage, leakage, blow-down, minor line pack fluctuations, and lost and unaccounted for natural gas

2.1 Beginning on the date on which deliveries of natural gas are commenced hereunder, and thereafter for the remaining term of this Agreement, Shipper agrees to tender gas to Transporter at the Point(s) of Receipt, and Transporter agrees to transport and redeliver and Shipper agrees to accept delivery of the Equivalent Quantities of gas at the Point(s) of Delivery, all &bbsp; in accordance with the terms of this Agreement

Notwithstanding the MDDO at each Point of Delivery, Shipper shall not nominate a total quantity of natural gas at all Points of Delivery that exceeds the MDQ set forth in this Agreement

Right Not to Be Subject to Automated Decisions Individuals have the right not to be subject to decisions based solely on automated processing — including profiling — that produce legal or similarly significant effects. Processing in violation of this right is unlawful under GDPR Article 22 and subject to supervisory authority enforcement and fines

High-Risk AI Systems Operators of high-risk AI systems as defined under the EU AI Act must comply with conformity assessment requirements before deployment. Placing a non-compliant high-risk AI system on the market is a serious violation subject to fines of up to €30 million or 6% of global annual turnover

Transparency Obligations Where automated decision-making is used, individuals must be provided with meaningful information about the logic involved, the significance, and the envisaged consequences. Failure to provide adequate transparency constitutes a violation of GDPR Article 13/14

Human Oversight High-risk AI systems must be designed to allow effective oversight by natural persons. Deployment of high-risk AI systems without adequate human oversight mechanisms violates the EU AI Act and may result in prohibition orders and financial penalties

Prohibited AI Practices Certain AI practices are prohibited outright under the EU AI Act, including subliminal manipulation, exploitation of vulnerabilities, and real-time remote biometric identification in public spaces with limited exceptions. Deployment of prohibited AI systems is a critical violation subject to the highest penalty tier
BIOMETRIC DATA COLLECTION AND USE POLICY Based on: Illinois Biometric Information Privacy Act (BIPA, 740 ILCS 14), GDPR Article 9, and CCPA Biometric Data provisions

Informed Consent Biometric identifiers — including fingerprints, retina scans, facial geometry, and voiceprints — must not be collected without first providing a written policy and obtaining written consent. Collection of biometric data without consent violates BIPA and subjects the entity to statutory damages of $1,000 per negligent violation and $5,000 per intentional or reckless violation, plus attorney fees

Retention Schedule Biometric data must be permanently destroyed when the initial purpose for collection has been satisfied or within 3 years of the individual's last interaction, whichever occurs first. Retention of biometric data beyond permitted periods violates BIPA and constitutes a separate, actionable violation per data subject per retention period

Prohibition on Sale or Profit Biometric data must not be sold, leased, traded, or otherwise profited from under any circumstances. Any commercial transaction involving biometric identifiers or information is a per se violation of BIPA and subjects the entity to significant statutory damages

Data Security Biometric data must be stored using a reasonable standard of care no less protective than the standard used to protect other confidential and sensitive information. Failure to adequately secure biometric data is an independent violation

Third-Party Disclosure Biometric data must not be disclosed or disseminated to third parties without the data subject's consent or as required by law. Any unauthorised third-party disclosure constitutes a violation and subjects the disclosing party to statutory damages
Consumer Rights California residents have the right to know what personal information is collected
Non-compliance may result in civil penalties of $2,500 per unintentional violation and $7,500 per intentional violation enforced by the Attorney General
Opt-Out Rights Consumers may opt out of the sale of personal information at any time. Businesses must honor opt-out requests immediately and may not deny services to consumers exercising this right

Data Security Businesses must implement reasonable security procedures. Failure to maintain adequate security is subject to civil litigation and statutory damages of $100 to $750 per consumer per incident
Minors Collection of data from children under 16 requires explicit opt-in consent. Violations involving minors' data attract enhanced penalties

Consent Requirement Analytics and marketing cookies require explicit user consent before activation. Deploying non-essential cookies without consent violates ePrivacy regulations and may result in regulatory fines and reputational damage

Third-Party Sharing Data collected via cookies shall not be shared with third parties without user consent. Unauthorized sharing constitutes a breach of applicable data protection laws and may result in enforcement action
Retention Limits Cookies must not be retained beyond their stated purpose or declared lifespan. Exceeding declared retention periods constitutes a policy violation subject to regulatory audit

Children's Protection Tracking cookies must not be deployed on pages directed at children under 13. Any violation of children's data protection requirements will result in mandatory reporting and enhanced regulatory scrutiny

Scope of Application This policy applies to operators of websites and online services directed to children under 13 years of age, and to operators with actual knowledge that they collect personal information from children under 13. Operators who fail to comply with COPPA are subject to civil penalties of up to $51,744 per violation

Verifiable Parental Consent Operators must obtain verifiable parental consent before collecting, using, or disclosing personal information from children. Collecting personal information from children without verifiable parental consent is a violation of the COPPA Rule and subject to FTC enforcement action and civil penalties

Privacy Notice Requirements Operators must post a clear and prominent privacy notice on their website describing what information is collected from children, how it is used, and disclosure practices. Failure to provide a compliant privacy notice is an independent COPPA violation

Data Retention and Deletion Personal information collected from children must be retained only as long as reasonably necessary to fulfil the purpose for which it was collected. Upon a parent's request, operators must delete all personal information about a child. Failure to delete upon valid request constitutes a violation

Prohibition on Behavioural Advertising Operators may not condition a child's participation in an activity on the collection of more personal information than is reasonably necessary for that activity. Conditioning access to services on excessive data collection is an unfair practice under COPPA and the FTC Act

Purpose Specification The purposes for which personal data is collected must be specified, explicit, and legitimate at the time of collection. Processing personal data for purposes not disclosed to individuals at the time of collection is a violation of purpose limitation and subject to supervisory authority enforcement and fines

Compatible Use Assessment Where personal data is to be used for a new purpose, a compatibility assessment must be documented. Processing for an incompatible new purpose without a fresh legal basis or consent is unlawful under GDPR Article 5(1)(b)

Data Minimisation Only personal data that is adequate, relevant, and limited to what is necessary in relation to the purposes of processing may be collected. Excessive data collection is a violation of the data minimisation principle subject to ICO investigation and enforcement action

Anonymisation and Pseudonymisation Where personal data is retained for statistical, research, or archival purposes, organisations should implement anonymisation or pseudonymisation to minimise privacy risks. Failure to implement available privacy-enhancing techniques may be an aggravating factor in the event of a data breach

Privacy Impact on Marketing and Analytics Use of personal data for profiling, behavioural advertising, or analytics must be limited to data that is necessary for the specific activity. Obtaining data for one purpose and repurposing it for marketing without consent or a compatible use assessment is a violation of both purpose limitation and data minimisation principles
DATA RETENTION AND SECURE DISPOSAL POLICY Based on: GDPR Article 5(1)(e), UK GDPR, and ISO 27001 Data Management Standards

Retention Schedule Compliance Personal data must be retained only for as long as necessary for the purposes for which it was collected. Organisations must maintain a documented retention schedule for all categories of personal data. Retention of personal data beyond scheduled periods without lawful basis is a violation of the storage limitation principle and subject to supervisory authority enforcement action

Secure Deletion When personal data reaches its scheduled retention end-date, it must be securely deleted or anonymised. Hard deletion must render data unrecoverable. Disposal methods must be documented and verifiable. Failure to securely delete personal data is a violation subject to ICO investigation

Backup Data Personal data held in backup systems is subject to the same retention obligations as live data. Organisations must implement processes to delete personal data from backups within a reasonable period of the scheduled retention end-date. Indefinite retention in backups is not compliant with GDPR storage limitation requirements

Subject Access and Deletion Requests On receipt of a valid erasure request under GDPR Article 17, personal data must be deleted without undue delay unless a retention exemption applies. Failure to process erasure requests is a violation of data subject rights subject to supervisory authority investigation and enforcement

Physical Media Disposal Physical media containing personal data must be disposed of securely using methods that prevent recovery, such as cross-cut shredding or degaussing. Placing media containing personal data in general waste is a serious data protection violation subject to significant fines and reputational damage
Data Collection Scope Employee personal data is collected only for lawful employment purposes. Collection of data beyond stated purposes is prohibited and constitutes a data protection violation
Monitoring Policy Electronic monitoring of employee communications requires prior notification. Covert monitoring without legal basis is prohibited and may expose the organisation to legal liability

Access Controls Employee data is accessible only to authorised HR personnel and direct managers. Unauthorised access to employee records constitutes a serious disciplinary offence and may result in termination

Retention After Termination Employee data shall be retained for no longer than 7 years after termination unless required by law. Data retained beyond this period without legal basis must be deleted upon request

Cookie Consent Non-essential cookies and similar tracking technologies must not be placed on a user's device without prior, freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent. Placing tracking cookies without valid consent is a violation of PECR. The ICO may issue fines of up to £500,000 and enforcement notices for PECR breaches

Consent Mechanisms Consent must be obtained through a clear affirmative action. Pre-ticked boxes, consent obtained through continued browsing, or consent bundled with terms of service does not constitute valid consent under PECR. Organisations using invalid consent mechanisms must remediate immediately upon notification

Direct Marketing Electronic marketing communications must only be sent to individuals who have specifically consented to receive them, or where the soft opt-in exemption applies. Sending unsolicited direct marketing without valid consent is a violation subject to ICO fines and enforcement action

Right to Withdraw Consent Individuals have the right to withdraw consent for cookies and direct marketing at any time. Withdrawal must be as easy as giving consent. Failure to honour withdrawal requests is a violation of PECR

Subscriber and Traffic Data Traffic and location data generated by electronic communications services must be erased or anonymised when no longer needed for transmission. Retention of traffic data beyond permitted periods is a violation of the ePrivacy Directive and PECR

Access Rights Students who are 18 or older (or enrolled in postsecondary education) have the right to inspect and review their education records within 45 days of a request. Denial of access without lawful grounds constitutes a FERPA violation. Institutions that violate FERPA may lose federal funding administered by the Department of Education

Consent for Disclosure Educational institutions must obtain written consent before disclosing education records to third parties. Disclosure without consent is prohibited except in specific circumstances permitted by FERPA. Unlawful disclosure constitutes a violation subject to complaint, investigation, and potential loss of federal funding

Directory Information Institutions may disclose designated directory information without consent only after notifying students and providing an opportunity to opt out. Disclosure of information that a student has opted out of having disclosed constitutes a FERPA violation

School Officials Exception Education records may be shared with school officials who have a legitimate educational interest without student consent. Officials must have a genuine need to review the record. Disclosure to officials without a legitimate educational interest is an unlawful disclosure under FERPA

Complaint and Enforcement Students or parents may file complaints with the Student Privacy Policy Office of the U.S. Department of Education. The Department may require corrective action. Continued non-compliance following a finding of violation may result in termination of federal funding to the institution
Retention Periods Personal data shall not be kept longer than necessary for its stated purpose
Violation of retention limits constitutes a breach under GDPR Article 5(1)(e) and may result in fines up to 20 million or 4% of annual global turnover

Right to Erasure Data subjects may request erasure of personal data without undue delay. Controllers must comply within 30 days. Non-compliance is subject to regulatory enforcement and administrative fines

Data Breach Notification Personal data breaches must be reported to supervisory authorities within 72 hours. Failure to notify constitutes an infringement attracting administrative fines and potential criminal liability

Cross-Border Transfers Transfer of personal data to third countries lacking adequate protections is prohibited. Unauthorized transfers may result in suspension of processing activities and significant financial penalties

Definition of Breach A breach is an impermissible use or disclosure of unsecured PHI that compromises its security or privacy. All impermissible uses or disclosures are presumed to be breaches unless the covered entity demonstrates a low probability that PHI has been compromised based on a four-factor risk assessment

Individual Notification Affected individuals must be notified without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 calendar days following discovery of a breach. Notification must be written and must describe the breach, the types of information involved, steps individuals should take, and what the entity is doing to investigate and mitigate harm

Media Notification For breaches affecting more than 500 residents of a state or jurisdiction, covered entities must notify prominent media outlets serving the affected area. Media notification must occur without unreasonable delay and within 60 days of discovery

HHS Notification All breaches must be reported to the Secretary of HHS. Breaches affecting 500 or more individuals must be reported within 60 days of discovery. Breaches affecting fewer than 500 individuals may be reported annually. Failure to report constitutes an independent violation of the HIPAA Rules

Penalties for Non-Notification Failure to provide required breach notifications subjects the covered entity to civil monetary penalties tiered by culpability: from $100 per violation for unknowing violations to $50,000 per violation for wilful neglect that is not corrected

Permitted Uses and Disclosures Protected health information (PHI) may only be used or disclosed for treatment, payment, or healthcare operations without patient authorisation. All other uses require a valid written authorisation from the individual. Disclosure of PHI for purposes beyond these categories without authorisation constitutes a violation of the HIPAA Privacy Rule and subjects the covered entity to civil and criminal penalties

Minimum Necessary Standard Covered entities must make reasonable efforts to limit PHI to the minimum necessary to accomplish the intended purpose. Disclosure of PHI beyond the minimum necessary is a regulatory violation subject to enforcement action by the HHS Office for Civil Rights (OCR)

Individual Rights Patients have the right to access, copy, and request amendment of their PHI. Covered entities must respond to access requests within 30 days. Denial of access without lawful grounds constitutes a violation and may be reported to OCR for investigation and civil monetary penalties of up to $50,000 per violation category

Breach Notification A breach of unsecured PHI must be reported to affected individuals without unreasonable delay and no later than 60 days after discovery. Breaches affecting 500 or more individuals must be reported to HHS and prominent media outlets. Failure to notify is an independent HIPAA violation subject to significant penalties

Business Associate Agreements Covered entities must enter into written business associate agreements (BAAs) with all vendors who access PHI. Use or disclosure of PHI by a business associate without a valid BAA constitutes a violation. Business associates are directly liable for compliance under the HITECH Act amendments

Administrative Safeguards Covered entities must implement a security management process including a risk analysis and risk management programme. Failure to conduct and document a risk analysis is the most frequently cited HIPAA Security Rule violation and subjects the entity to civil monetary penalties

Physical Safeguards Facilities housing electronic PHI (ePHI) must implement physical access controls, workstation use policies, and device and media controls. Unauthorised physical access to systems containing ePHI constitutes a serious violation subject to corrective action and financial penalties

Technical Safeguards Covered entities must implement access controls, audit controls, integrity controls, and transmission security for ePHI. Transmission of ePHI over open networks without encryption is a violation. Entities must implement automatic logoff and unique user identification for all systems accessing ePHI

Workforce Training All workforce members with access to ePHI must receive regular security awareness training. Failure to provide documented training is a compliance deficiency subject to audit findings and enforcement

Incident Response Security incidents involving ePHI must be identified, responded to, and documented. Failure to have and implement incident response procedures is a violation. All security incident documentation must be retained for a minimum of six years
Legal Bases for Processing Personal data may only be processed on one of the ten legal bases established by

of the LGPD. Processing without a valid legal basis is unlawful. The Brazilian Data Protection Authority (ANPD) may apply administrative sanctions including fines of up to 2% of revenue in Brazil, limited to R$50 million per infraction

Data Subject Rights Data subjects have the right to confirm the existence of processing, access their data, correct inaccurate data, anonymise or delete unnecessary data, obtain portability, and object to processing. Controllers must respond to requests within 15 days. Failure to respond or unlawful denial of rights is subject to ANPD investigation and sanctions

Sensitive Personal Data Processing of sensitive personal data — including health data, biometric data, racial or ethnic origin, religious conviction, and political opinion — requires explicit and specific consent or another legal basis permitted by Article 11 LGPD. Unlawful processing of sensitive data attracts enhanced scrutiny and sanctions

Data Protection Officer Organisations processing personal data must appoint a Data Protection Officer (DPO). The DPO's identity and contact details must be publicly disclosed. Failure to appoint or disclose a DPO is a violation subject to administrative warning

Cross-Border Data Transfers Personal data may only be transferred internationally to countries with adequate protection or where appropriate guarantees are in place. Transfers without adequate safeguards are prohibited and subject to sanctions including suspension of processing
PIPEDA — CANADA PERSONAL INFORMATION PROTECTION COMPLIANCE POLICY Based on: Personal Information Protection and Electronic Documents Act (S.C. 2000, c. 5)

Consent Requirement Organisations must obtain meaningful consent before collecting, using, or disclosing personal information. Consent obtained through deceptive or misleading practices is not valid. Failure to obtain valid consent is a violation of PIPEDA and may result in an OPC investigation and Federal Court orders requiring compliance

Limiting Collection Only information necessary for the identified purposes must be collected. Collection of personal information beyond identified purposes is a violation of the limiting collection principle and subject to OPC investigation and findings of non-compliance

Breach of Security Safeguards Since November 2018, organisations must report breaches of security safeguards that pose a real risk of significant harm to the Privacy Commissioner of Canada and notify affected individuals. Failure to report a qualifying breach is an offence subject to fines of up to $100,000 CAD

Access and Correction Rights Individuals have the right to know what personal information an organisation holds about them and to challenge its accuracy. Organisations must respond to access requests within 30 days. Unjustified denial of access or correction rights is subject to OPC complaint investigation and Federal Court proceedings

Accountability Organisations are responsible for personal information under their control, including information disclosed to third parties for processing. Organisations must implement contractual protections with third parties. Failure to maintain accountability for third-party processing is a violation of PIPEDA's accountability principle

Conditions for Lawful Processing Personal information may only be processed if it is adequate, relevant, and not excessive; collected for a specific purpose; and processed in a manner compatible with that purpose. Unlawful processing may result in enforcement notices, fines of up to R10 million, or imprisonment of up to 10 years for responsible parties

Security Safeguards Responsible parties must secure the integrity and confidentiality of personal information by taking appropriate, reasonable technical and organisational measures. Failure to implement adequate security safeguards resulting in a compromise of personal information is a serious violation of POPIA

Notification of Security Compromises Where there are reasonable grounds to believe that personal information has been accessed or acquired by an unauthorised person, the Information Regulator and the data subject must be notified as soon as reasonably possible. Failure to notify is a violation subject to enforcement action

Direct Marketing Personal information may not be used for direct marketing by electronic means without the data subject's consent. Sending direct marketing communications without consent is a violation of POPIA and subject to enforcement action by the Regulator

Transborder Information Flows Personal information may not be transferred to a foreign country unless that country provides an adequate level of protection or the data subject consents. Unlawful cross-border transfers are subject to enforcement action and penalties

Controller-Processor Contracts Where personal data is processed by a third party on behalf of the organisation, a written Data Processing Agreement (DPA) must be in place before processing commences. Engaging a processor without a compliant DPA is a violation of GDPR Article 28 and subject to supervisory authority enforcement action and fines

Processor Due Diligence Controllers must only appoint processors that provide sufficient guarantees of appropriate technical and organisational measures. Engaging a processor without adequate due diligence is a violation of GDPR accountability requirements

Sub-Processing Processors must not engage sub-processors without prior specific or general written authorisation from the controller. Unauthorised sub-processing is a violation of the DPA and GDPR Article 28(2), giving rise to liability for both processor and controller

Joint Controller Arrangements Where two or more controllers jointly determine the purposes and means of processing, a transparent arrangement must be documented in writing. Failure to establish and communicate a joint controller arrangement is a violation of GDPR Article 26

Data Sharing Agreements Personal data shared with third-party controllers for independent processing requires a lawful basis and, where required, a data sharing agreement. Unlawful sharing of personal data with third parties is a serious violation subject to the highest penalty tier under GDPR

Internal Reporting All staff who become aware of a potential personal data breach must report it to the Data Protection Officer (DPO) immediately and no later than within 24 hours of discovery. Failure to report internally may result in disciplinary action and could prevent the organisation from meeting its regulatory notification obligations

ICO Notification Personal data breaches that are likely to result in a risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals must be reported to the ICO within 72 hours of becoming aware of the breach. Failure to notify within 72 hours without good reasons is a violation subject to ICO reprimand and fines of up to £8.7 million or 2% of global turnover

Individual Notification Where a breach is likely to result in a high risk to the rights and freedoms of individuals, those individuals must be notified without undue delay. Failure to notify affected individuals when required constitutes a serious violation subject to enforcement action

Documentation All breaches, whether notifiable or not, must be documented in the organisation's breach register. Failure to maintain adequate breach records is a violation of the UK GDPR accountability principle

Post-Breach Review Following any significant breach, the organisation must conduct a root cause analysis and implement appropriate remedial measures. Failure to take remedial action following a breach may be treated as an aggravating factor in any subsequent ICO investigation

Lawful Basis for Processing All processing of personal data must have a lawful basis under Article 6 UK GDPR. Processing without a lawful basis is unlawful. The ICO may issue enforcement notices, assessment notices, and fines of up to £17.5 million or 4% of global annual turnover, whichever is higher, for serious violations

Data Subject Rights Data subjects have the right to access, rectify, erase, restrict, and port their personal data. Controllers must respond to requests without undue delay and within one calendar month. Failure to respond within the required timeframe is a violation subject to ICO investigation and enforcement action

Data Protection by Design Controllers must implement data protection by design and by default. This requires appropriate technical and organisational measures from the outset of any processing activity. Failure to implement data protection by design is a violation subject to reprimand and fines

Data Protection Impact Assessments A DPIA is mandatory before undertaking processing likely to result in high risk to individuals. Failure to conduct a required DPIA is a violation. Where a DPIA indicates high residual risk, the ICO must be consulted before processing commences

International Transfers Personal data must not be transferred to a country outside the UK unless that country ensures an adequate level of data protection or appropriate safeguards are in place. Unlawful international transfers are subject to enforcement action and fines
